learning_objectives: Define project management and the project lifecycle
Identify the five process groups: initiating, planning, executing, monitoring/controlling, closing
Explain the role and competencies of a project manager

lecture_notes.introduction: Project management is the application of knowledge, skills, tools, and techniques to project activities to meet requirements. In a world where more work is organized as projects, PM skills are essential.
key_concepts: Projects vs. operations: temporary vs. ongoing
Triple constraint: scope, time, cost (and quality)
Five process groups and their interactions
Project lifecycle phases: initiation, planning, execution, closure
PM knowledge areas: scope, schedule, cost, quality, resource, communications, risk, procurement, stakeholders
lecture_notes.demonstration: Chartering a project: developing a project charter for a real-world scenario including business case, objectives, constraints, and stakeholder identification
setup_requirements: Project management software (MS Project, Asana, or equivalent)

review.value: Scope, time, and cost are interdependent; changing one affects the others. Quality is at the center. The PM's job is managing these tradeoffs.
Formal document authorizing the project; includes business case, objectives, high-level requirements, milestones, budget, and sponsor authorization

learning_objectives: Write clear requirements and scope statements
Manage scope creep and change control

lecture_notes.introduction: Scope management ensures the project includes all the work required and only the work required. The WBS is the foundation of all project planning.
key_concepts: Requirements gathering: interviews, workshops, prototypes
Scope statement: deliverables, acceptance criteria, exclusions
WBS: hierarchical decomposition of total scope into work packages
100% rule: WBS must capture ALL deliverable work
Scope creep: uncontrolled changes; managed through change control process
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a complete WBS for a product launch project: decomposing from major deliverables to work packages

review.value: Hierarchical decomposition of total project scope into manageable work packages; the basis for schedule, cost, and resource planning
Uncontrolled expansion of project scope without adjustments to time, cost, or resources; the #1 cause of project failure

class_schedule.topic: Schedule Management: CPM and Gantt Charts
learning_objectives: Estimate activity durations using multiple techniques
Construct a network diagram and calculate the critical path

lecture_notes.introduction: Schedule management turns the WBS into a time-based plan. The critical path method identifies which activities determine the project's minimum duration.
key_concepts: Activity sequencing: dependencies (FS, FF, SS, SF)
Duration estimation: analogous, parametric, three-point (PERT)
Network diagram: AON (Activity on Node)
Critical path: longest path through network; determines minimum duration
Float/slack: how much an activity can slip without delaying the project
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a network diagram for a 15-activity project, calculating forward and backward pass, identifying critical path and float

review.value: The sequence of activities with zero float; any delay on the critical path delays the entire project by the same amount
Expected duration = (Optimistic + 4*Most Likely + Pessimistic) / 6; accounts for uncertainty in activity duration

class_schedule.topic: Cost Management and Earned Value Analysis
learning_objectives: Develop project cost estimates and budgets
Apply Earned Value Management to track project performance
Calculate and interpret CPI and SPI

lecture_notes.introduction: Cost management ensures the project is completed within the approved budget. Earned Value Management provides an objective, quantitative method for measuring project performance.
key_concepts: Cost estimation: bottom-up, analogous, parametric
Budget: cost baseline + management reserves
Earned Value: PV (Planned Value), EV (Earned Value), AC (Actual Cost)
Performance indices: CPI = EV/AC, SPI = EV/PV
Forecasting: EAC = BAC/CPI, ETC = EAC - AC
lecture_notes.demonstration: EVM exercise: given project status data, calculating EV metrics, diagnosing whether the project is over budget and/or behind schedule, and forecasting completion cost

review.value: CPI = 0.85 means getting $0.85 of value for every $1 spent; the project is 15% over budget relative to work completed
SPI = 0.90 means 90% of planned work has been completed; the project is 10% behind schedule

learning_objectives: Identify, analyze, and prioritize project risks
Develop risk response strategies: avoid, mitigate, transfer, accept
Create stakeholder engagement strategies based on power-interest analysis

lecture_notes.introduction: Risk management is about being proactive: identifying what could go wrong before it does and having a plan ready. Stakeholder management ensures the right people are engaged at the right level.
key_concepts: Risk identification: brainstorming, checklists, expert judgment, SWOT
Qualitative analysis: probability-impact matrix
Quantitative analysis: expected monetary value, Monte Carlo simulation
Risk response strategies: avoid, mitigate, transfer, accept (for threats); exploit, enhance, share, accept (for opportunities)
Power-interest matrix for stakeholder classification
lecture_notes.demonstration: Risk workshop: identifying and analyzing risks for a construction project, building a risk register, and designing response strategies

review.value: Living document listing all identified risks with probability, impact, priority, response strategy, owner, and trigger indicators
High power/high interest: manage closely. High power/low interest: keep satisfied. Low power/high interest: keep informed. Low power/low interest: monitor

class_schedule.topic: Agile Project Management and Hybrid Approaches
learning_objectives: Apply Scrum framework: sprints, ceremonies, artifacts
Design hybrid approaches combining predictive and agile elements

lecture_notes.introduction: Not all projects are the same. Agile approaches work best when requirements are uncertain and change is expected. Many organizations use hybrid approaches combining predictive and agile elements.
key_concepts: Agile Manifesto: individuals, working software, collaboration, responding to change
Scrum: product backlog, sprint planning, daily standup, sprint review, retrospective
Kanban: visualize workflow, limit WIP, manage flow
When to use predictive vs. agile vs. hybrid
Scaling agile: SAFe, LeSS frameworks
lecture_notes.demonstration: Sprint simulation: running a mini-sprint (20 minutes) with backlog grooming, planning, execution, and retrospective

review.value: Sprint Planning (what to do), Daily Standup (sync), Sprint Review (demo), Retrospective (improve); time-boxed and regular
Predictive: well-defined requirements, stable scope, sequential phases. Agile: evolving requirements, frequent delivery, iterative cycles

questions.question: A project has the following activities and durations (in days): A(3), B(5), C(4), D(6), E(2). Dependencies: A->C, A->D, B->D, C->E, D->E. Draw the network and calculate the critical path and project duration.
questions.answer: Network paths: A(3)->C(4)->E(2) = 9 days. A(3)->D(6)->E(2) = 11 days. B(5)->D(6)->E(2) = 13 days. Critical path: B->D->E = 13 days (longest path). Float on A->C->E = 13-9 = 4 days. Float on A->D->E = 13-11 = 2 days.

rubric.key: Correct network diagram with all paths
Correct project duration and float calculations

questions.question: A client requests adding three new features mid-project that were not in the original scope. The team wants to accommodate. Explain the proper change control process and why simply adding the work without it is dangerous.
questions.answer: Proper change control: (1) Log the change request formally in the change log. (2) Analyze the impact on scope, schedule, cost, quality, and risk. (3) Present the analysis to the change control board (or sponsor) showing the tradeoffs: 'Adding these features will extend the timeline by 3 weeks and increase budget by $30K.' (4) Get formal approval or rejection. (5) If approved, update the project baseline (scope, schedule, budget). Simply adding work without this process is scope creep: the project takes longer and costs more than planned, but the baseline doesn't change, so the PM appears to be failing. It also sets a precedent that any stakeholder can add work without accountability for the impact.

rubric.key: Explains why bypassing it is dangerous

questions.question: A project has BAC of $200,000. At month 6, PV=$120,000, EV=$100,000, AC=$130,000. Calculate CPI, SPI, EAC, and interpret the project's health.
questions.answer: CPI = EV/AC = $100K/$130K = 0.77. SPI = EV/PV = $100K/$120K = 0.83. EAC = BAC/CPI = $200K/0.77 = $259,740. Interpretation: The project is in trouble on both dimensions. CPI of 0.77 means getting only $0.77 of value per dollar spent (23% over budget). SPI of 0.83 means only 83% of planned work is complete (17% behind schedule). At this rate, the project will cost $259,740, approximately $60K over the original budget. Corrective action is urgently needed.

questions.question: Explain the difference between risk mitigation and risk transfer, and give a business example of each.
questions.answer: Risk mitigation reduces the probability or impact of a risk through proactive action. Example: a software project mitigates the risk of key-person dependency by cross-training team members and documenting processes, so if someone leaves, the project can continue. Risk transfer shifts the financial consequence of a risk to a third party. Example: a construction company purchases insurance to transfer the financial risk of weather damage, or uses a fixed-price contract to transfer cost overrun risk to a vendor. The risk event can still occur, but the financial impact is borne by another party.

major_assignments.title: Comprehensive Project Management Plan
major_assignments.description: Develop a complete project management plan for a realistic project scenario including project charter, WBS, schedule (CPM and Gantt), budget, risk register, stakeholder analysis, and communication plan.
objectives: Create a professional project charter with business justification
Build a complete WBS decomposed to work package level
Develop a network diagram with critical path analysis
Produce a comprehensive risk register and stakeholder management plan
deliverables: Complete WBS with dictionary entries for work packages
Network diagram with CPM analysis and Gantt chart

criteria: WBS dictionary entries for key work packages
Bottom-up cost estimate linked to WBS
Communication plan addresses all stakeholder groups
Would be usable by a real project team
Accurate schedule with critical path analysis
Comprehensive risk and stakeholder management plans

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of PM concepts and quantitative techniques

hands_on_exercises.description: In-class exercises using PM tools and techniques

midterm_examination.description: Covers scope, schedule, and cost management

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam including risk, stakeholders, and agile approaches

course_policies.attendance: Required for hands-on exercises and sprint simulation. Agile exercises are collaborative and cannot be made up.
course_policies.late_submissions: PM plan milestones penalized 10% per day. Gantt chart and CPM exercises must be submitted on time.
course_policies.collaboration: PM plan may be completed in teams of 2-3. Quizzes and exams are individual.
course_policies.academic_integrity: PM plans must be original work. Using templates is acceptable but must be substantially customized.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Project management software (MS Project, Monday.com, or Asana). Excel for EVM calculations. Gantt chart tools.
